summary: Kenneth demonstrates how to sell covered calls on Meta using Thinkorswim, earning $1,800 premium by Friday, but advises against this specific trade due to upcoming earnings and stock volatility risk. He illustrates the capital requirement ($46,000 for 100 Meta shares) and contrasts returns across different stock prices (Palantir example yields only $13).
takeaways: Avoid selling covered calls on stocks down dramatically when earnings are imminent—risk of assignment outweighs premium collected
Strike prices further out-of-the-money yield smaller premiums but lower assignment probability
Capital requirement: $46,600 needed to sell one covered call contract on Meta at $466/share to collect $1,800 premium
Covered call premium collection requires owning 100 shares per contract
Lower-priced stocks like Palantir ($27-28 range) generate minimal weekly premium (~$13/contract) vs. expensive stocks like Meta/Tesla
One-week expiration on covered calls during earnings week is too risky despite attractive premium dollar amounts

strategy_candidates.change: Focus covered call selling on earnings-free weeks and avoid stocks down significantly that could pop on positive catalysts
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly states 'when a stock is down dramatically, it's not the best time to sell covered calls because the stock could surge' and notes Meta's upcoming Wednesday earnings makes Friday expiration risky—this directly contradicts Brian's portfolio goal of consistent $1,250/mo and avoids early assignment losses

title: Here’s how to make money selling covered calls
